_schema_notes: Read this before adding entries. Each entry describes a known interaction between two substances (supplements and/or medications). The checker matches user-entered substances against the 'a' and 'b' fields (case-insensitive, and against 'aliases') and surfaces the entry if both are present in the user's stack. DO NOT add an entry without a real, checkable source in 'sources'. DO NOT use 'severity: high' unless the source material itself characterizes it that way. Every new entry must follow this exact shape — do not invent new top-level fields without updating this notes block to explain why.

schema.a: string, primary name of substance A, lowercase
schema.b: string, primary name of substance B, lowercase
schema.aliases_a: array of strings, alternate names/spellings for A
schema.aliases_b: array of strings, alternate names/spellings for B
schema.severity: one of: 'low', 'moderate', 'high' — only use a level the source material itself supports
schema.mechanism: string, plain-language explanation of WHY this interaction happens, not just that it does
schema.summary: string, 1-3 sentences, hedge appropriately ('may', 'is associated with'), no dosage or start/stop instructions
schema.sources: array of objects: { title, url, publisher }. At least one required. Prefer NIH ODS, peer-reviewed journals, or established drug-interaction references.
schema.last_reviewed: ISO date string, set when entry is added or last verified

entries.mechanism: Warfarin works by inhibiting vitamin K-dependent clotting factors. Vitamin K intake directly opposes this mechanism, which can reduce warfarin's anticoagulant effect.
entries.summary: Vitamin K can reduce the ability of warfarin to prevent blood clotting. People on warfarin are typically advised to keep their vitamin K intake consistent rather than avoiding it entirely, since sudden changes (increases or decreases) are what destabilize INR control.

sources.title: Dietary Supplements: What You Need to Know
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: St. John's Wort induces the cytochrome P450 3A4 enzyme system, which speeds up the metabolism (breakdown) of many drugs, including warfarin, lowering their effective blood levels.
entries.summary: St. John's Wort has been associated with decreased anticoagulation effect and unstable INR values when taken with warfarin, due to faster drug metabolism.

sources.title: Clinically relevant safety issues associated with St. John's wort product labels
sources.publisher: PMC / peer-reviewed

entries.mechanism: St. John's Wort increases serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine availability through reuptake inhibition, similar to SSRIs. Combined with an SSRI, this can push serotonin levels too high.
entries.summary: Combining St. John's Wort with SSRI antidepressants may dangerously elevate serotonin levels, with a risk of serotonin syndrome, a serious condition. This combination is generally considered one to actively avoid rather than just monitor.

sources.title: The Effects of St. John's Wort and its Interactions with SSRI's
sources.publisher: European Psychiatry / peer-reviewed

entries.mechanism: St. John's Wort induces CYP3A4 metabolism, which can lower circulating levels of the hormones in oral contraceptives.
entries.summary: St. John's Wort may reduce the effectiveness of oral contraceptives, with case reports describing breakthrough bleeding and unplanned pregnancy associated with concurrent use.

sources.title: Clinically relevant safety issues associated with St. John's wort product labels
sources.publisher: PMC / peer-reviewed

entries.mechanism: Some chemotherapy drugs work partly by generating oxidative stress in cancer cells. High-dose antioxidant supplements could theoretically counteract this mechanism, though research on real-world clinical impact is mixed.
entries.summary: Antioxidant supplements such as vitamins C and E might reduce the effectiveness of some types of cancer chemotherapy. Anyone undergoing chemotherapy should discuss any supplement use with their oncology team before starting or continuing.

sources.title: Dietary Supplements: What You Need to Know
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Iron forms insoluble complexes (chelates) with levothyroxine in the gastrointestinal tract, which reduces how much of the thyroid drug is absorbed into the bloodstream.
entries.summary: Iron supplements may reduce the absorption of levothyroxine, potentially decreasing its effectiveness in managing thyroid conditions. This interaction is noted in levothyroxine prescribing information.

sources.title: Iron — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Calcium binds to bisphosphonate molecules in the gastrointestinal tract, forming insoluble complexes that significantly reduce bisphosphonate absorption.
entries.summary: Calcium supplements may substantially reduce the absorption of bisphosphonate medications used for osteoporosis. This interaction is recognized in bisphosphonate prescribing guidelines.

sources.title: Calcium — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Magnesium (along with other divalent cations like calcium, zinc, and iron) chelates fluoroquinolone antibiotics in the GI tract, forming poorly absorbed complexes and reducing the antibiotic's bioavailability.
entries.summary: Magnesium supplements may significantly reduce the absorption of fluoroquinolone antibiotics, potentially lowering their effectiveness. Antacids containing magnesium have similar effects and are explicitly noted in fluoroquinolone prescribing information.

sources.title: Magnesium — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) can inhibit platelet aggregation through effects on thromboxane and prostacyclin production. Combined with warfarin's anticoagulant action, this may produce an additive effect on bleeding tendency.
entries.summary: Omega-3 fatty acids may have additive effects with warfarin on bleeding risk. The clinical evidence on whether fish oil supplements meaningfully change INR is mixed, but the combination is noted as a potential interaction in anticoagulant management guidelines. People on warfarin who take fish oil supplements should inform their prescribing provider.

sources.title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Vitamin D enhances intestinal calcium absorption. Thiazide diuretics reduce urinary calcium excretion by the kidneys. Both effects together raise circulating calcium levels, and the combination may lead to hypercalcemia — elevated blood calcium.
entries.summary: Combining vitamin D supplements with thiazide diuretics may increase the risk of hypercalcemia (high blood calcium), because both raise calcium levels through different mechanisms. This interaction is recognized in vitamin D prescribing information and is noted in the NIH ODS vitamin D fact sheet.

sources.title: Vitamin D — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: ACE inhibitors and angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs) both reduce aldosterone secretion, which decreases the kidney's ability to excrete potassium. Taking supplemental potassium on top of this impaired excretion can lead to hyperkalemia — elevated blood potassium levels, which can affect heart rhythm.
entries.summary: Potassium supplements combined with ACE inhibitors or ARBs may cause hyperkalemia (high blood potassium), which can disrupt heart rhythm. Both drug classes increase potassium retention through the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, so additional supplemental potassium warrants monitoring. This interaction is documented in the NIH ODS potassium fact sheet.

sources.title: Potassium — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Garlic (allicin and related organosulfur compounds) inhibits platelet aggregation and may enhance fibrinolytic activity. Combined with warfarin's anticoagulant action, the additive effect on bleeding tendency may increase bleeding risk.
entries.summary: Garlic supplements may have additive effects with warfarin on bleeding risk through platelet inhibition. The NIH ODS garlic fact sheet notes this interaction and recommends that people on anticoagulants inform their provider about garlic supplement use.

sources.title: Garlic — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Zinc, like magnesium and calcium, is a divalent cation (Zn²⁺) that chelates fluoroquinolone antibiotics in the gastrointestinal tract, forming poorly absorbed complexes. This reduces the antibiotic's bioavailability and may lower its effectiveness against infection.
entries.summary: Zinc supplements may significantly reduce the absorption of fluoroquinolone antibiotics through chelation in the GI tract. The NIH ODS zinc fact sheet documents this interaction; the effect is similar to calcium and magnesium interacting with the same antibiotic class.

sources.title: Zinc — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Ginkgo biloba contains ginkgolides that inhibit platelet activating factor (PAF), reducing platelet aggregation. This antiplatelet effect, combined with warfarin's anticoagulant action, may produce an additive increase in bleeding risk.
entries.summary: Ginkgo biloba may have additive effects with warfarin on bleeding risk due to its antiplatelet properties. The NCCIH notes this interaction and advises caution when combining ginkgo with anticoagulants or antiplatelet drugs.

sources.title: Ginkgo — Herbs at a Glance
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Potassium-sparing diuretics work by blocking aldosterone receptors (spironolactone, eplerenone) or by directly blocking epithelial sodium channels in renal tubules (amiloride, triamterene), both of which reduce urinary potassium excretion. Adding supplemental potassium to this already-impaired excretion can cause clinically significant hyperkalemia.
entries.summary: Potassium supplements taken with potassium-sparing diuretics may cause hyperkalemia (dangerously elevated blood potassium), which can disrupt heart rhythm. Potassium-sparing diuretics already substantially reduce potassium excretion; supplemental potassium may push levels further. This interaction is documented in the NIH ODS potassium fact sheet.

sources.title: Potassium — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Zinc is a divalent cation that forms insoluble chelate complexes with tetracycline antibiotics in the gastrointestinal tract, substantially reducing antibiotic absorption. This is the same chelation mechanism by which magnesium, calcium, and iron reduce antibiotic absorption.
entries.summary: Zinc supplements may significantly reduce the absorption of tetracycline antibiotics — including doxycycline and minocycline — by forming chelate complexes in the GI tract. This interaction is documented in the NIH ODS zinc fact sheet and is recognized in tetracycline prescribing information.

sources.title: Zinc — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Calcium ions bind to levothyroxine in the gastrointestinal tract, forming insoluble complexes that reduce how much of the thyroid hormone is absorbed into the bloodstream. This is the same chelation mechanism through which iron reduces levothyroxine absorption.
entries.summary: Calcium supplements may reduce the absorption of levothyroxine, potentially decreasing its effectiveness for thyroid conditions. The NIH ODS calcium fact sheet notes this interaction; the standard advice is to separate levothyroxine from calcium supplements.

sources.title: Calcium — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Melatonin promotes sleep by acting on MT1 and MT2 receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus, suppressing the brain's alerting signal and lowering core body temperature. This CNS-depressant effect is additive with other sedating substances — benzodiazepines, non-benzodiazepine sleep aids, sedating antihistamines, opioids, and alcohol — all of which independently suppress CNS function through different mechanisms.
entries.summary: Melatonin combined with CNS depressants such as benzodiazepines, sedative-hypnotics, or alcohol may produce additive sedation, impaired coordination, or cognitive blunting. This is a pharmacodynamic interaction: both substances suppress CNS function independently, and the effects add. The NIH ODS melatonin fact sheet documents this interaction category.

sources.title: Melatonin — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Garlic supplements can induce CYP3A4, the major hepatic enzyme responsible for metabolizing many drugs including HIV protease inhibitors. CYP3A4 induction increases the rate of drug metabolism, reducing plasma concentrations of the affected medication. Pharmacokinetic studies have shown garlic supplements reduce saquinavir plasma concentrations by approximately 51% — a clinically significant reduction for an antiretroviral where maintaining plasma levels is essential for viral suppression.
entries.summary: Garlic supplements may substantially reduce plasma concentrations of HIV protease inhibitors (especially saquinavir) by inducing CYP3A4-mediated metabolism. A roughly 50% reduction in saquinavir levels has been documented in pharmacokinetic studies. NCCIH and NIH ODS both flag this interaction. People on antiretroviral regimens including protease inhibitors should avoid garlic supplements.

sources.title: Garlic
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

sources.title: Garlic — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Fluvoxamine is a potent inhibitor of CYP1A2, the primary liver enzyme responsible for metabolizing melatonin. When CYP1A2 is inhibited, melatonin clearance slows dramatically, and plasma melatonin levels can increase approximately 17-fold compared to taking melatonin alone.
entries.summary: Fluvoxamine substantially increases melatonin blood levels by blocking CYP1A2, the enzyme that normally clears melatonin. The NIH ODS melatonin fact sheet documents approximately a 17-fold rise in plasma melatonin with this combination. This can substantially amplify melatonin's sedating effects.

sources.title: Melatonin — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Ginkgo biloba contains ginkgolide B, a potent platelet-activating factor (PAF) antagonist that inhibits platelet aggregation. Antiplatelet drugs such as aspirin (COX-1 inhibitor) and clopidogrel (P2Y12 receptor antagonist) block platelet activation through different pathways. Combining ginkgo with these drugs produces additive antiplatelet inhibition across multiple mechanisms simultaneously.
entries.summary: Ginkgo biloba may have additive antiplatelet effects when combined with aspirin, clopidogrel, or similar antiplatelet drugs, potentially increasing bleeding risk. NCCIH notes this interaction and recommends caution when combining ginkgo with any anticoagulant or antiplatelet medication.

sources.title: Ginkgo — Herbs at a Glance
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: St. John's Wort strongly induces CYP3A4, a major hepatic enzyme involved in metabolizing several statins — particularly simvastatin and lovastatin, which are almost entirely dependent on CYP3A4. Induction increases the rate of statin metabolism, lowering plasma statin concentrations and reducing their cholesterol-lowering effect.
entries.summary: St. John's Wort may reduce the effectiveness of simvastatin and lovastatin by inducing CYP3A4, the enzyme responsible for metabolizing these statins. NCCIH flags this interaction. Atorvastatin is also partially CYP3A4-dependent and may be affected to a lesser degree. Pravastatin and rosuvastatin are not CYP3A4-dependent and are less likely to be affected.

sources.title: St. John's Wort
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Red yeast rice contains monacolin K, a compound chemically identical to lovastatin — a prescription statin. Taking red yeast rice alongside a prescription statin is pharmacologically equivalent to taking two statins simultaneously, multiplying the effective dose and the associated risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.
entries.summary: Red yeast rice contains monacolin K, which is chemically identical to the prescription statin lovastatin. Combining it with any statin medication results in an unintended additive statin dose, substantially increasing the risk of muscle damage (myopathy or rhabdomyolysis). NCCIH flags this as a serious interaction.

sources.title: Red Yeast Rice
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Ginkgo seeds contain ginkgotoxin (4-O-methylpyridoxine), a vitamin B6 antagonist that can lower seizure threshold. Although most commercial ginkgo leaf extracts contain minimal ginkgotoxin, case reports of seizures in people with pre-existing seizure disorders suggest ginkgo may destabilize seizure control.
entries.summary: Ginkgo biloba use has been associated with seizures, particularly in people with pre-existing seizure disorders. NCCIH recommends that people taking antiepileptic medications use caution with ginkgo and disclose its use to their prescriber.

sources.title: Ginkgo — Herbs at a Glance
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: High doses of vitamin E (above approximately 400 IU/day) inhibit platelet aggregation and may interfere with vitamin K-dependent clotting factor activity, creating additive bleeding risk when combined with warfarin's anticoagulant effect.
entries.summary: High-dose vitamin E supplements may increase bleeding risk when combined with warfarin. The NIH ODS Vitamin E fact sheet notes that large supplemental doses can inhibit platelet aggregation and may potentiate the anticoagulant effect of warfarin. Dietary amounts of vitamin E are unlikely to be clinically significant.

sources.title: Vitamin E — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: High doses of vitamin E inhibit platelet aggregation — partly by interfering with thromboxane A2 synthesis — through a mechanism that is additive with, but distinct from, that of antiplatelet drugs such as aspirin (COX-1 inhibitor) and clopidogrel (P2Y12 antagonist). The combined effect is broader inhibition of platelet function across multiple pathways.
entries.summary: High-dose vitamin E supplements may have additive antiplatelet effects when combined with aspirin, clopidogrel, or similar antiplatelet drugs, potentially increasing bleeding risk. The NIH ODS Vitamin E fact sheet notes that large supplemental doses can inhibit platelet aggregation. This interaction is most relevant at supplement doses well above typical dietary intake.

sources.title: Vitamin E — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Metformin impairs vitamin B12 absorption by interfering with calcium-dependent uptake of the intrinsic factor–B12 complex at the ileal membrane. This is a pharmacodynamic depletion: metformin does not interact with B12 directly, but reduces how much is absorbed from food over time. The effect is dose-dependent and accumulates over months to years of treatment.
entries.summary: Long-term metformin use is associated with reduced vitamin B12 absorption and may lead to B12 deficiency over time. The NIH ODS Vitamin B12 fact sheet notes this interaction and that B12 blood levels should be monitored in people on long-term metformin. Supplementing with B12 during metformin therapy may be warranted, particularly in people with other B12 risk factors.

sources.title: Vitamin B12 — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, the enzyme that controls the mevalonate pathway. This pathway produces not only cholesterol but also ubiquinone (CoQ10), a molecule central to mitochondrial energy production. By blocking the pathway, statins reduce the body's endogenous CoQ10 synthesis and may lower circulating CoQ10 levels.
entries.summary: Statins may reduce circulating CoQ10 levels by inhibiting the mevalonate pathway, which the body uses to produce both cholesterol and CoQ10. NCCIH notes this relationship. Whether this reduction contributes to statin-associated muscle symptoms is debated; clinical trial evidence on CoQ10 supplementation for muscle symptoms has been mixed. Taking CoQ10 alongside statins is not contraindicated.

sources.title: Coenzyme Q10
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Zinc and iron are both divalent cations that share the same intestinal transporter (DMT1, also called SLC11A2). When taken together in large amounts, they compete for absorption at this transporter, so high doses of one can meaningfully reduce absorption of the other. This is most significant when both are taken simultaneously at supplemental (not dietary) doses.
entries.summary: High-dose zinc and iron supplements may reduce each other's absorption when taken at the same time, due to competition at the DMT1 intestinal transporter. The NIH ODS zinc fact sheet documents this competition. Separating them by at least two hours minimizes the interaction.

sources.title: Zinc — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: High-dose zinc intake induces the production of metallothionein in intestinal epithelial cells. Metallothionein binds copper with higher affinity than zinc, trapping copper inside the enterocyte and preventing its absorption into systemic circulation. Over weeks to months at high supplemental zinc doses (typically 50 mg/day or more), this can progressively deplete copper stores.
entries.summary: Sustained high-dose zinc supplementation can cause copper deficiency by inducing metallothionein, which sequesters copper in intestinal cells and prevents its absorption. The NIH ODS zinc fact sheet documents this interaction. This effect typically requires prolonged high-dose zinc intake rather than short-term use.

sources.title: Zinc — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Berberine activates AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), a cellular energy-sensing enzyme that increases glucose uptake in muscle and liver cells and suppresses hepatic glucose production — a mechanism similar to metformin. This blood glucose-lowering effect is additive with diabetes medications that lower glucose through the same or different pathways, including metformin, sulfonylureas, and insulin.
entries.summary: Berberine may have additive blood glucose-lowering effects when combined with diabetes medications such as metformin, sulfonylureas, or insulin, potentially increasing the risk of hypoglycemia. NCCIH notes that berberine can lower blood sugar and flags interaction risk with antidiabetic drugs.

sources.title: Berberine
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Berberine inhibits CYP3A4, a major hepatic enzyme responsible for metabolizing a wide range of drugs. As a CYP3A4 inhibitor, berberine slows the breakdown of drugs that depend on this enzyme, raising their plasma concentrations and prolonging their effects. This is the opposite of St. John's Wort's CYP3A4 induction. Cyclosporine, an immunosuppressant with a narrow therapeutic window, is among the best-documented cases of this interaction.
entries.summary: Berberine inhibits CYP3A4, which can increase plasma concentrations of medications metabolized by that enzyme, including cyclosporine and some statins. NCCIH notes this interaction risk, particularly with immunosuppressants and other drugs with narrow therapeutic windows. People taking CYP3A4-sensitive medications should consult their prescriber before using berberine.

sources.title: Berberine
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Curcumin, the primary bioactive constituent of turmeric, inhibits platelet aggregation by suppressing thromboxane B2 synthesis and reducing collagen-induced platelet activation. These antiplatelet effects are additive with warfarin's anticoagulant action, potentially increasing bleeding risk beyond what INR monitoring would reflect, since antiplatelet activity is not captured by INR.
entries.summary: Turmeric supplements may increase bleeding risk when combined with warfarin or other anticoagulants due to curcumin's antiplatelet properties. NCCIH notes the interaction and flags caution for people on anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy. The antiplatelet component operates through a different mechanism than warfarin, so INR alone may not fully reflect the combined effect.

sources.title: Turmeric
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Curcumin inhibits CYP1A2 and CYP3A4 enzymes in the liver. These cytochrome P450 enzymes are responsible for metabolizing a broad range of medications. As an inhibitor, curcumin slows drug breakdown, raising plasma concentrations and prolonging the effective presence of the affected drug — which can amplify both therapeutic effects and side effects.
entries.summary: Curcumin may inhibit CYP1A2 and CYP3A4 enzymes, raising plasma levels of medications that depend on these pathways for clearance. NCCIH notes that turmeric supplements may interact with drugs metabolized by the cytochrome P450 system. People taking medications with narrow therapeutic windows — such as tacrolimus, cyclosporine, or certain anticoagulants — should consult their prescriber.

sources.title: Turmeric
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Ashwagandha has been shown in some clinical studies to increase levels of thyroid hormones, particularly T4 (thyroxine) and T3 (triiodothyronine), and to alter TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) secretion. People on thyroid replacement therapy are already managing a narrow target range; any additional stimulation of thyroid hormone levels can shift them outside that range, requiring dose adjustment.
entries.summary: Ashwagandha may increase thyroid hormone levels (T4 and T3), which could amplify the effect of thyroid medications and push hormone levels outside the therapeutic range. NCCIH notes that ashwagandha may interact with thyroid hormone medications. People on levothyroxine or other thyroid treatments should monitor thyroid function if using ashwagandha.

sources.title: Ashwagandha
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Ashwagandha has immunomodulatory effects — it can stimulate both innate and adaptive immune activity, including increasing natural killer cell activity and white blood cell counts. In people taking immunosuppressant medications (e.g., after an organ transplant or for autoimmune conditions), this immune stimulation may work directly against the drug's intended effect of keeping immune activity suppressed.
entries.summary: Ashwagandha may stimulate immune system activity in ways that counteract immunosuppressant medications used after organ transplantation or for autoimmune conditions. NCCIH notes this interaction risk. People on immunosuppressants should avoid ashwagandha without guidance from their transplant team or prescriber.

sources.title: Ashwagandha
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Ashwagandha has anxiolytic and sedative properties, likely involving modulation of GABA-A receptor activity by withanolide constituents. This CNS-depressant effect is pharmacodynamically additive with other sedating substances — benzodiazepines, non-benzodiazepine sleep aids, alcohol, and gabapentinoids — each of which independently suppresses CNS function through overlapping or complementary pathways.
entries.summary: Ashwagandha may have additive sedating effects when combined with CNS depressants such as benzodiazepines, sedative-hypnotics, or alcohol. NCCIH notes that ashwagandha may interact with medications that cause sleepiness and recommends caution with CNS depressants.

sources.title: Ashwagandha
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Valerenic acid, the primary bioactive constituent of valerian root, inhibits GABA transaminase and modulates GABA-A receptors, producing anxiolytic and sedative effects through GABAergic mechanisms. This is pharmacodynamically additive with CNS depressants — benzodiazepines, barbiturates, non-benzodiazepine hypnotics, and alcohol — all of which enhance GABAergic inhibition or suppress CNS function through related pathways.
entries.summary: Valerian root may have additive sedating effects when combined with CNS depressants such as benzodiazepines, sedative-hypnotics, or alcohol due to overlapping GABAergic mechanisms. NCCIH notes that valerian may interact with sedatives and other CNS depressants, and recommends disclosing its use to healthcare providers.

sources.title: Valerian
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Echinacea preparations stimulate innate immune function — increasing phagocytic activity, natural killer cell activity, and cytokine production (including TNF-alpha and interleukins). In people taking immunosuppressant medications to prevent transplant rejection or manage autoimmune disease, this immune stimulation directly opposes the drug's intended effect of suppressing immune activity.
entries.summary: Echinacea may stimulate immune system activity in ways that counteract immunosuppressant medications used after organ transplantation or for autoimmune conditions. NCCIH notes that people taking immunosuppressants should avoid echinacea. The immune stimulation could theoretically precipitate rejection or disease flare.

sources.title: Echinacea
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Black cohosh inhibits CYP2D6, the liver enzyme responsible for converting tamoxifen to endoxifen — its primary active metabolite that actually exerts the anti-estrogenic effect in breast cancer treatment. Reduced CYP2D6 activity from black cohosh may lower endoxifen plasma concentrations and potentially reduce tamoxifen's therapeutic efficacy.
entries.summary: Black cohosh may inhibit CYP2D6, reducing the conversion of tamoxifen to its active metabolite endoxifen and potentially diminishing tamoxifen's effectiveness in breast cancer treatment. NCCIH notes that black cohosh may interact with hormone-sensitive conditions and treatments. People taking tamoxifen or other hormone-modulating cancer therapies should discuss black cohosh use with their oncologist.

sources.title: Black Cohosh
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Kavalactones, the active compounds in kava, modulate GABA-A receptor activity and interact with serotonin and dopamine receptors, producing anxiolytic and sedative effects. These CNS-depressant properties are pharmacodynamically additive with benzodiazepines, barbiturates, non-benzodiazepine hypnotics, and alcohol — all of which enhance GABAergic inhibition or suppress CNS function through related pathways. Combined use can produce greater sedation, cognitive impairment, and in higher exposures, risk of respiratory depression.
entries.summary: Kava may have additive sedating effects when combined with benzodiazepines, sedative-hypnotics, alcohol, or other CNS depressants due to overlapping GABAergic mechanisms. NCCIH notes that kava has CNS depressant properties and can interact with substances that cause drowsiness. People taking sedating medications should avoid combining them with kava without medical guidance.

sources.title: Kava
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Kava has been associated with hepatotoxicity — cases of serious liver injury, including fulminant hepatic failure, have been documented and prompted regulatory action in several countries. The mechanism is not fully established but may involve reactive metabolites from cytochrome P450-mediated kava metabolism and inhibition of multiple CYP enzymes. When kava is used alongside other medications that carry hepatotoxic risk, the combined burden on the liver may exceed that of either agent alone.
entries.summary: Kava has been associated with serious liver injury, including fulminant hepatic failure, in documented case reports. Combining kava with other potentially hepatotoxic medications — such as high-dose acetaminophen, statins, or antifungal azoles — may increase the risk of liver damage. NCCIH flags kava's hepatotoxicity risk prominently and notes that people with liver disease or taking hepatotoxic drugs should avoid kava.

sources.title: Kava
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) and other catechins in green tea form stable complexes with non-heme iron in the GI tract, rendering it less absorbable. This chelation is most significant when green tea or high-dose EGCG supplements are consumed simultaneously with iron-containing foods or iron supplements. The effect is primarily on non-heme (plant-derived) iron rather than heme iron from meat.
entries.summary: Green tea catechins, particularly EGCG, may reduce the absorption of non-heme iron by forming insoluble complexes with it in the GI tract. NCCIH notes that green tea may interact with iron. The interaction is most clinically relevant for people with iron deficiency or those taking iron supplements; separating intake by at least an hour reduces it.

sources.title: Green Tea
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Glycyrrhizin, a triterpene compound in licorice root, inhibits 11-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2 (11-beta-HSD2) — the enzyme that inactivates cortisol in the kidney tubule. With this enzyme inhibited, cortisol accumulates and activates mineralocorticoid receptors, producing effects resembling excess aldosterone: sodium and water retention, potassium excretion, and elevated blood pressure. This pseudo-hyperaldosteronism state directly counteracts antihypertensive medications and worsens potassium loss from loop and thiazide diuretics.
entries.summary: Licorice root containing glycyrrhizin may raise blood pressure by causing sodium retention and potassium loss through 11-beta-HSD2 inhibition, counteracting antihypertensive medications and worsening diuretic-related potassium depletion. NCCIH notes that licorice root may interact with antihypertensives, corticosteroids, and diuretics. Deglycyrrhizinated licorice (DGL) does not carry this risk, as glycyrrhizin has been removed.

sources.title: Licorice Root
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Saw palmetto has been associated with antiplatelet activity in case reports and some in vitro evidence. Antiplatelet effects are additive with anticoagulant and antiplatelet medications — warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel, and direct oral anticoagulants — potentially increasing bleeding risk. The clinical evidence base is more limited than for supplements with well-characterized antiplatelet activity such as garlic or ginkgo biloba, but the interaction is plausible and flagged by NCCIH.
entries.summary: Saw palmetto may have antiplatelet effects that could increase bleeding risk when combined with anticoagulants or antiplatelet medications such as warfarin, aspirin, or clopidogrel. NCCIH notes the potential interaction with blood-thinning medications. People taking anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy should inform their prescriber about saw palmetto use, particularly before surgery.

sources.title: Saw Palmetto
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Silymarin, the active flavonolignan complex in milk thistle, inhibits cytochrome P450 enzymes CYP2C9 and CYP3A4, as well as P-glycoprotein and organic anion transporting polypeptides (OATPs). CYP2C9 metabolizes warfarin, many NSAIDs, and certain antidiabetic drugs; CYP3A4 metabolizes a very large proportion of common medications including immunosuppressants, statins, benzodiazepines, and many others. Inhibiting these enzymes slows drug metabolism, raising plasma levels of affected medications.
entries.summary: Milk thistle (silymarin) may inhibit CYP2C9 and CYP3A4, raising plasma concentrations of medications metabolized through these pathways, including warfarin, cyclosporine, tacrolimus, and certain statins. NCCIH notes that milk thistle may interact with medications changed by the liver. The interaction is of greatest concern with narrow-therapeutic-index drugs.

sources.title: Milk Thistle
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Proanthocyanidins in grape seed extract inhibit platelet aggregation through multiple pathways including suppression of thromboxane A2 synthesis and inhibition of collagen-induced platelet activation. These antiplatelet effects are additive with anticoagulant and antiplatelet medications. Some evidence also suggests grape seed extract may inhibit CYP3A4 at high doses, potentially raising plasma levels of medications metabolized through that enzyme.
entries.summary: Grape seed extract has antiplatelet properties that may increase bleeding risk when combined with warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel, or direct oral anticoagulants. NCCIH notes that grape seed extract may interact with blood-thinning medications. People on anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy should disclose grape seed extract use to their healthcare provider.

sources.title: Grape Seed Extract
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Parthenolide and other sesquiterpene lactones in feverfew inhibit platelet aggregation by suppressing collagen-induced platelet activation and inhibiting arachidonic acid metabolism. These antiplatelet effects are additive with warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel, and direct oral anticoagulants. Feverfew also modulates serotonin release from platelets and may affect prostaglandin synthesis.
entries.summary: Feverfew's parthenolide inhibits platelet aggregation, which may increase bleeding risk when combined with anticoagulants or antiplatelet medications such as warfarin, aspirin, or clopidogrel. NCCIH notes that feverfew may interact with blood-thinning medications. People taking anticoagulant therapy should inform their prescriber about feverfew use.

sources.title: Feverfew
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Passionflower contains flavonoids including chrysin that modulate GABA-A receptors — the same receptor system targeted by benzodiazepines, z-drugs (zolpidem, eszopiclone), and barbiturates. GABA-A modulation produces sedative and anxiolytic effects, and combining passionflower with other CNS depressants produces additive central nervous system depression, increasing sedation, cognitive impairment, and respiratory depression risk.
entries.summary: Passionflower may enhance the sedative effects of benzodiazepines, z-drugs, opioids, alcohol, and other CNS depressants through additive GABA-A modulation. NCCIH notes that passionflower may interact with sedative medications. People taking prescription sleep aids, anti-anxiety medications, or opioids should use caution with passionflower supplements.

sources.title: Passionflower
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: 5-HTP is the direct metabolic precursor to serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine). It crosses the blood-brain barrier and is converted to serotonin by aromatic amino acid decarboxylase. SSRIs block the reuptake transporter that clears serotonin from the synapse, increasing serotonin availability. Combining 5-HTP with an SSRI elevates serotonin through two independent mechanisms simultaneously — increased synthesis plus reduced clearance — which can push serotonin activity to dangerous levels, producing serotonin syndrome.
entries.summary: Combining 5-HTP with SSRIs may cause serotonin syndrome — a potentially serious condition characterized by agitation, tremor, rapid heart rate, and in severe cases, hyperthermia and seizures. NCCIH notes this as a significant interaction. The combination elevates serotonin through both increased production (5-HTP) and reduced clearance (SSRI reuptake inhibition), and is generally contraindicated.

sources.title: 5-Hydroxytryptophan (5-HTP)
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) block the enzyme that breaks down serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine. 5-HTP directly increases serotonin synthesis by providing its immediate precursor. The combination produces a severe excess of serotonin through simultaneous overproduction and blocked degradation — a high-risk scenario for serotonin syndrome, which can be fatal.
entries.summary: Combining 5-HTP with MAOIs is high-risk for serotonin syndrome due to simultaneous increased serotonin synthesis (5-HTP) and blocked serotonin breakdown (MAOI). This combination is generally contraindicated. NCCIH flags this interaction. The risk applies to irreversible MAOIs (phenelzine, tranylcypromine), selective MAO-B inhibitors (selegiline, rasagiline), and linezolid (an antibiotic with MAOI properties).

sources.title: 5-Hydroxytryptophan (5-HTP)
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: CoQ10 (coenzyme Q10) has a structural similarity to vitamin K2 and may mildly support the vitamin K-dependent clotting pathway, potentially reducing warfarin's anticoagulant effect. Case reports and some clinical data document reduced INR (increased clotting tendency) in patients taking CoQ10 alongside warfarin. The mechanism is not fully established — the structural analogy to vitamin K is the leading hypothesis but alternative mechanisms have not been ruled out.
entries.summary: CoQ10 supplementation has been associated with reduced warfarin effectiveness in case reports and some clinical studies, potentially lowering INR and reducing anticoagulation. NCCIH notes that CoQ10 may interact with warfarin. People on warfarin who start or stop CoQ10 supplementation should monitor INR more frequently.

sources.title: Coenzyme Q10
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Rhodiola rosea extracts, particularly salidroside and rosavins, have demonstrated mild monoamine oxidase (MAO) inhibitory activity in preclinical studies. Combining rhodiola with prescription MAOIs could amplify MAO inhibition, potentially producing excess serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine. NCCIH notes that rhodiola may interact with drugs affecting monoamine systems.
entries.summary: Rhodiola may have mild MAO-inhibiting properties that could be additive with prescription MAOIs, potentially contributing to excess monoamine neurotransmitter levels. The clinical significance of this interaction is not well-characterized, but the theoretical basis for additive MAO inhibition warrants caution. Anyone taking phenelzine, tranylcypromine, selegiline, rasagiline, or linezolid should discuss rhodiola use with a prescriber.

sources.title: Rhodiola
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Rhodiola rosea acts as an adaptogen with stimulant-like properties. It appears to influence dopaminergic and noradrenergic neurotransmission, reducing fatigue and improving physical and cognitive performance. Combined with stimulant medications (methylphenidate, amphetamines, modafinil) or high-dose caffeine, the additive stimulation may increase cardiovascular effects including heart rate and blood pressure, and heighten anxiety or insomnia.
entries.summary: Rhodiola has stimulant-like properties that may be additive with prescription stimulants or high-dose caffeine, potentially increasing cardiovascular effects and anxiety. NCCIH notes caution with combining rhodiola with stimulant medications. The interaction is considered moderate rather than high-severity because rhodiola's stimulant mechanism is indirect and adaptogenic rather than a direct CNS stimulant like amphetamines.

sources.title: Rhodiola
sources.publisher: National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH)

entries.mechanism: Magnesium is a cofactor for insulin receptor tyrosine kinase and plays a central role in insulin signaling. The NIH ODS Magnesium fact sheet documents that magnesium deficiency is associated with impaired insulin secretion and action, and that adequate magnesium may enhance insulin sensitivity. High-dose magnesium supplementation could theoretically compound the blood glucose-lowering effect of insulin and sulfonylureas; however, the clinical evidence for this interaction causing significant hypoglycemia is limited and most effects are modest.
entries.summary: Magnesium plays a role in insulin signaling and glucose metabolism. High-dose supplementation may modestly enhance insulin sensitivity, which could add to the glucose-lowering effect of antidiabetic medications. The NIH ODS notes this relationship, though clinically significant hypoglycemia from this combination is uncommon. People managing diabetes who use magnesium supplements should monitor blood glucose, particularly at higher supplemental doses.

sources.title: Magnesium — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements

entries.mechanism: Ascorbic acid reduces ferric iron (Fe³⁺) to ferrous iron (Fe²⁺), the form transported across the intestinal wall by the DMT1 divalent metal transporter. Ascorbic acid also forms a chelate with iron in the gut lumen that remains soluble at the higher pH of the duodenum, preventing precipitation and keeping iron bioavailable for absorption. Together these effects can increase non-heme iron absorption 2- to 4-fold when vitamin C is consumed simultaneously with an iron supplement.
entries.summary: Vitamin C substantially enhances non-heme iron absorption by reducing ferric to ferrous iron and maintaining solubility in the gut. Taking iron supplements with vitamin C increases absorption, which is beneficial for people with iron deficiency but warrants attention for those with hemochromatosis or other iron overload conditions. Per the NIH ODS Vitamin C fact sheet, ascorbic acid is a recognized enhancer of non-heme iron absorption.

sources.title: Vitamin C — Health Professional Fact Sheet
sources.publisher: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements
